text: The surgeon uses a 5mm thoracic grasper positioned anteriorly on the left side, while the camera is placed more posteriorly. The right hand holds a Maryland dissector with bipolar energy, and the left hand uses soft-tipped Catiae forceps.

The surgeon uses a swab in the chest cavity for safety and cleaning small amounts of blood. The dissection progresses past the pulmonary vein, revealing the left pulmonary artery. The vagus nerve is gently grasped to preserve it, with visible pulmonary branches of the vagus nerve below the cariae. These branches are divided, and the vagus nerve is retracted posteriorly.

The surgeon dissects down the left main bronchus, revealing the collapsed cyst sac due to its connection to the airways under negative pressure. The Maryland dissector is initially used, followed by scissors to achieve fresh edges on the bronchus, minimizing thermal damage. The right main bronchus is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon opens the cyst, revealing its depth and a clean cystic cavity. The small hole in the membranous portion of the left main bronchus is visible, resembling the preoperative bronchoscopic view. Robotic scissors are used to perform the final excision, and the cyst is prepared for histological examination.

The surgeon mobilizes a small amount of mediastinal tissue to cover a hole in the bronchus, opting for a piece of native tissue instead of a larger pericardial patch. A single 4-0 Vicryl suture is used to secure the tissue in place.

The surgeon places two stitches into the membranous part of the bronchus to close the defect, applying Evisil glue over the wound for additional security. A drain is then positioned to complete the procedure.

The surgeon performs a long line of intercostal blocks and a phrenic nerve block at the start of the procedure. At the end of the case, the lungs are gently inflated with twenty centimeters of water pressure, resulting in full lung inflation, marking the completion of the cyst excision.
